1: A theme: Adonizedek ("my lord is righteousness") fears the spread of YHWH's kingdom and gathers opposition. The principalities and powers recognize the advance of God's reign and mount resistance — this pattern culminates in the cross, where the "ruler of this world" mobilized all opposition against Christ (John 12:31; 1 Corinthians 2:8: "none of the rulers of this age understood it, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory").
2: A theme: Gibeon's defection alarms the coalition — one city switching allegiance to YHWH's cause weakens the whole system of opposition. In the same way, every individual who comes to Christ is a defection from the kingdom of darkness and an alarm to the powers that oppose God's purposes (Colossians 1:13: "he has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves").
3: A theme: Adonizedek rallies four kings to mount a punitive expedition against Gibeon. The enemy's response to covenant conversion is always violence or pressure to return — those who come to Christ face opposition precisely because their conversion is a threat to the kingdom of darkness. The five Amorite kings represent organized, systemic opposition to YHWH's covenant purposes.
4: A theme: "It has made peace with Joshua and the Israelites" — Gibeon's peace treaty with Israel is the reason for the attack. The peace that Christ brings — "peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ" (Romans 5:1) — similarly provokes opposition from those who benefit from enmity. Christ said "I did not come to bring peace, but a sword" (Matthew 10:34) — not because peace is not his goal but because the arrival of true peace disrupts false peace.
5: A theme: the five Amorite kings besiege Gibeon — those who sought covenant life are immediately threatened. The new believer who transfers allegiance to Christ faces exactly this: "In fact, everyone who wants to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted" (2 Timothy 3:12). The siege of Gibeon is the universal pattern of the world's response to covenant conversion.
6: A type: "Do not abandon your servants! Come up to us quickly — rescue us and help us!" The Gibeonites' cry to their covenant lord is the prayer of the church under pressure. Christ is the suzerain who answers the vassal's cry: "I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you" (John 14:18). The cry for help from those who have found covenant shelter is always answered by the covenant lord.
7: A revelation of God: Joshua responds immediately — "marched up from Gilgal with his entire fighting force, including all his best warriors." YHWH's response to his people's need is total: "If God is for us, who can be against us?... He who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, graciously give us all things?" (Romans 8:31-32). The divine warrior commits everything to the rescue of his covenant people.
8: A direct revelation: "Do not be afraid of them; I have given them into your hand." The pre-battle divine guarantee recurs throughout Joshua's campaign — the certainty of victory precedes the battle. Christ's resurrection was this same pre-battle guarantee: God "raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand" (Ephesians 1:20), guaranteeing the final victory over every power before the battle is finished in history.
9: A theme: the overnight forced march — all-out effort, no holding back — to rescue the covenant people under siege. Christ's mission from the Father was similarly single-minded and total: "I have come down from heaven not to do my will but to do the will of him who sent me" (John 6:38). The complete commitment of the suzerain to rescue his vassal is a figure of Christ's incarnation as total commitment to his people's redemption.
10: A revelation of God: YHWH "threw them into panic" before Israel struck a blow — the divine action precedes and enables the human. In the spiritual battle, the victory over sin and death was accomplished by YHWH's action in raising Christ (Colossians 2:12-13), not by human spiritual effort. The panic that disintegrates the enemy's formation is the work of God, not human strategy.
11: A revelation of God: "More of them were killed by the hailstones than by the Israelites' swords." The divine contribution to victory exceeds the human. Paul's warfare language emphasizes the same asymmetry: "the weapons we fight with are not the weapons of the world. On the contrary, they have divine power to demolish strongholds" (2 Corinthians 10:4). YHWH himself is the primary warrior; his people are instruments.
12: A revelation of God: Joshua commands the sun and moon, and they obey — creation at the service of redemption. Christ demonstrates this same authority over creation: "he got up and rebuked the winds and the waves, and it was completely calm" (Matthew 8:26). The natural order responds to the commands of YHWH's servant because all creation was made through and for Christ (Colossians 1:16).
13: A shadow: the Book of Jashar preserved the account of YHWH's unprecedented intervention. The preservation of testimony to YHWH's acts of salvation — in written form, in the covenant community — points toward the Scripture's own role as preserved testimony. "These things are written that you may believe that Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name" (John 20:31).
14: A revelation of God: "There has never been a day like it — a day when the LORD listened to a human voice." The incarnation is the ultimate "day when YHWH listens to a human voice" — the Son of God prays as a human being, and the Father responds. At Gethsemane, Calvary, and the resurrection, the dialogue between the divine and human is at its most intense. Christ's cry from the cross ("My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?") and his resurrection vindication are the definitive divine-human encounter that the long day anticipates.
15: A theme: the return to Gilgal — the covenant renewal site — after the battle. The recurring return to the place of covenant commitment frames the campaign. In the Christian life, every battle is fought from the place of covenant assurance — baptism, the Lord's Table, the word of God — and returns to it. The covenant center is both the launch point and the resting point.
16: A theme: the five kings flee and hide in a cave — the powers of darkness retreating from YHWH's advancing kingdom. "The one who is in you is greater than the one who is in the world" (1 John 4:4). The hiding of defeated kings in a cave anticipates the final defeat of every power: "they called to the mountains and the rocks, 'Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who sits on the throne'" (Revelation 6:16).
17: A theme: the kings are found hiding — nothing is hidden from YHWH. "Nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account" (Hebrews 4:13). The defeated powers cannot escape the final reckoning.
18: A theme: "Roll large stones to the entrance of the cave and post guards" — contain and proceed. The stone blocking the cave entrance is an ironic foreshadowing: a stone sealed an enemy's tomb at Joshua's command (v27), as a stone was sealed over Christ's tomb — only for Christ to emerge from his in resurrection, reversing the pattern (Matthew 27:66; Joshua 28:2).
19: A revelation of God: "Don't you stay there! Pursue your enemies; cut off their retreat." The divine warrior strategy is relentless pursuit — no half-measures. This reflects the thoroughness of Christ's salvation: he "appeared once for all at the culmination of the ages to do away with sin by the sacrifice of himself" (Hebrews 9:26). The pursuit is total; the victory is meant to be complete.
20: A theme: "The survivors having taken refuge in the fortified cities" — even after a devastating defeat, some resistance persists. The Christian life is honest about the ongoing presence of sin and opposition after the decisive victory of the cross: "although the body is dead because of sin, the Spirit gives life" (Romans 8:10). The final mopping-up of remaining enemies is the work of the age between Christ's first and second coming.
21: A revelation of God: "No one dared to say a word against the Israelites" — the complete psychological and political collapse of opposition after YHWH's intervention. "At the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth" (Philippians 2:10). The intimidated silence of all opposition before YHWH's advancing kingdom anticipates the universal acknowledgment of Christ's lordship.
22: A theme: the kings are brought out of the cave — exposed, brought to light. "He who does evil hates the light and will not come into the light for fear that his deeds will be exposed" (John 3:20). The exposure of the hidden powers is part of Christ's work: "he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross" (Colossians 2:15).
23: A revelation of God: the five kings represent the major power centers of the Canaanite world — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon. Their public defeat and execution signals the end of the old order. Christ's death and resurrection similarly defeat the "ruler of this world" (John 12:31) — not merely individuals but the systemic powers of sin, death, and the accuser.
24: A direct fulfillment (forward): "Put your feet on the necks of these kings." This enacts Psalms 110:1: "Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet." The military ritual of foot-on-neck is the human enactment of a divine promise that the NT applies directly to Christ (Matthew 22:44; Acts 2:35; Hebrews 1:13, Joshua 10:13). "He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet" (1 Corinthians 15:25).
25: A direct revelation: "Do not be afraid or discouraged. Be strong and courageous, for this is how the LORD will deal with all the enemies you fight." Joshua's commissioning words to his commanders mirror Christ's Great Commission: "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me... And surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age" (Matthew 28:18-20). The assurance that YHWH's pattern of victory extends to all future battles is the same assurance Christ gives the church.
26: A type with direct NT fulfillment: the kings are executed and hung on five trees. This is the explicit type that Galatians 3:13 fulfills: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written: 'Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree'" (Deuteronomy 21:22-23). Joshua's hanging of defeated enemy kings anticipates the one who became a curse in the place of his enemies, bearing on the cross what his people deserved.
27: A type: the bodies taken down from the trees at sunset, buried in the same cave — the cave of defeat becomes a tomb. Christ's body was taken down from the cross before the Sabbath (John 19:31), fulfilling the Deuteronomy 21:23 requirement exactly. But where the Canaanite kings remain in their cave forever, Christ emerges from his tomb on the third day. The pattern of burial in the place of defeat is transformed by resurrection.
28: A theme: the systematic conquest formula — "struck with the sword... completely destroyed... treated as he had treated Jericho" — describes the comprehensive elimination of each center of resistance. Paul describes this kind of systematic spiritual conquest: "we take captive every thought to make it obedient to Christ" (2 Corinthians 10:5). The thoroughness of the campaign is the model for Christ's claim on every domain of existence.
29: A theme: Libnah — the campaign moves west, expanding the territory of YHWH's rule. The gospel's geographical spread — "Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, and to the ends of the earth" (Acts 1:8) — follows this pattern of systematic expansion from a center outward.
30: A revelation of God: "The LORD also handed Libnah and its king over to Israel" — YHWH as the subject of every conquest. The recurring naming of YHWH as the one who "hands over" the enemy emphasizes divine agency in every advance of the kingdom. Every advance of the gospel is likewise YHWH's work: "The Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved" (Acts 2:47).
31: A revelation of God: Lachish falls in two days. YHWH's timing is precise — not one day less, not one day more. God's sovereignty over the timing of every event in his redemptive plan is reflected throughout: "when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son" (Galatians 4:4). The two-day detail signals providential precision, not chance.
32: A theme: the second day capture of Lachish. The recurring "second day" and "third day" details in the conquest narrative embed the temporal patterns that reach their fulfillment in Christ's death, burial, and third-day resurrection — the definitive "second/third day" event.
33: A theme: Gezer attempts to help Lachish — the worldly system of mutual reinforcement among opposed powers. Christ defeats not just individual sins but the entire system: "having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross" (Colossians 2:15). The systematic defeat of the coalition's support structures corresponds to Christ's defeat of the principalities.
34: A theme: the Eglon siege — the surrounding of the city that cuts off retreat. Paul describes the comprehensive scope of Christ's work in similar terms: "so that through the church, the manifold wisdom of God should be made known to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly realms" (Ephesians 3:10). The church is the advancing siege around the remaining strongholds of darkness.
35: A theme: "That same day" captures — the speed of divine action when the time is right. "See, now is the acceptable time; see, now is the day of salvation" (2 Corinthians 6:2). God's decisive acts in history come when they come, not according to human scheduling.
36: A revelation of God: Hebron — the city of the giants, the place where the spies despaired — falls to YHWH's army under Joshua. What the previous generation feared most becomes the theater of God's greatest victory. Christ engages and defeats what is most feared — death itself, "the last enemy" (1 Corinthians 15:26) — precisely where defeat seemed most certain.
37: A theme: the destruction of Hebron and its surrounding towns. The comprehensive scope of the victory — city and dependent towns — reflects the comprehensive scope of Christ's salvation: "the earth is the LORD's, and everything in it" (Psalms 24:1). Nothing in the domain of sin and death is exempt from Christ's reclaiming work.
38: A theme: Debir ("the Word") captured. The city named for the inner sanctuary and divine speech falls to Joshua. In Christ, all that was hidden in the inner sanctuary — "the mystery hidden for ages" (Colossians 1:26) — is now made known. The Word of God who was hidden is now revealed in the incarnation.
39: A theme: the compliance formula — "as he had treated Hebron and Libnah" — establishes consistency of principle across every conquest. Christ's salvation is consistent across every person: "there is no difference between Jew and Gentile — the same Lord is Lord of all and richly blesses all who call on him" (Romans 10:12). The same Lord applies the same terms to every territory claimed.
40: A revelation of God: "He left no survivors; he devoted all who breathed to destruction, as the LORD God of Israel had commanded." The totality of the ḥērem points toward the final judgment — the complete elimination of everything that opposes YHWH's reign. "Nothing impure will ever enter it [the new Jerusalem]" (Revelation 21:27). The conquest's totality is an enacted prophecy of the new creation's purity.
41: A theme: the geographic summary — from Kadesh-barnea to Gaza, from Goshen to Gibeon — marks the extent of the southern campaign. Christ's commission reaches the uttermost parts of the earth (Acts 1:8). The geographic comprehensiveness of Joshua's southern campaign is a type of the gospel's universal reach.
42: A direct revelation: "Joshua captured all these kings and their lands in one campaign, because the LORD, the God of Israel, fought for Israel." "In one campaign" points toward the cross as the one decisive act that accomplishes what no extended campaign could: "he has appeared once for all at the culmination of the ages to do away with sin by the sacrifice of himself" (Hebrews 9:26). A single event, a single sacrifice, a single campaign — sufficient for all.
43: A theme: the return to Gilgal — the covenant center — after every campaign phase. The church returns again and again to the covenant center — the word, the sacraments, the assembly — as the anchor of every advance and the resting place after every battle.
